claim_a.text: ACE's targeted turn-by-turn feedback significantly improved negotiation performance between trials (t=2.97, p=0.003, d=0.38), while zero-shot GPT-4 feedback produced no significant improvement
claim_a.paper_title: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System

claim_b.text: Zero-shot GPT-4 feedback failed to improve human negotiator performance, with no significant difference from the no-feedback control condition
claim_b.paper_title: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System

label: support
notes: Both from ACE. Both confirm the same conclusion: zero-shot GPT-4 feedback does not help humans, while structured ACE feedback does. Same direction.

claim_a.text: AI negotiation coaching is most effective for psychological preparation outcomes like fear reduction and willingness to initiate, rather than tactical skill improvement
claim_a.paper_title: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?

claim_b.text: LLM-based coaching (ACE) produced significant objective improvement in negotiation deal outcomes (lower prices achieved) across transfer to a novel scenario, demonstrating tactical skill transfer
claim_b.paper_title: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System

label: contradiction
notes: Trucey vs. ACE. Trucey: AI coaching helps psychology not tactics. ACE: AI coaching improves objective deal prices. Opposite conclusions about whether AI coaching improves tactical skill.

claim_a.text: Participants who trained with Rehearsal's simulated conflict practice reduced their use of competitive strategies by 67% and doubled cooperative strategies in a real unaided conflict
claim_a.paper_title: Rehearsal: Simulating Conflict to Teach Conflict Resolution

claim_b.text: Simulated conflict practice does not significantly improve strategy recognition or recall compared to a lecture control group
claim_b.paper_title: Rehearsal: Simulating Conflict to Teach Conflict Resolution

label: nuance
notes: Both from Rehearsal. Practice improves application (behavior in real conflict) but not recognition/recall. Different DVs, both true.

claim_a.text: Standard instruction-following LLMs produce simulated conflict responses that are too agreeable and quickly capitulate to user viewpoints, making them ineffective as practice partners
claim_a.paper_title: Rehearsal: Simulating Conflict to Teach Conflict Resolution

claim_b.text: LLM negotiation agents tend to be overly agreeable and are not hardball bargainers, responding passively to users rather than driving the negotiation
claim_b.paper_title: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System

label: support
notes: Rehearsal and ACE independently identify the same LLM failure mode: excessive agreeableness as a practice partner. Same conclusion, different domains.

claim_a.text: The negotiation dataset was collected from 50 MBA students conducting used car negotiations, transcribed using OpenAI's Whisper API with manual edits
claim_a.paper_title: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System

claim_b.text: A theory-driven AI coach achieved the largest reduction in negotiation-related fear among all conditions tested
claim_b.paper_title: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?

label: unrelated
notes: ACE dataset collection method vs. Trucey fear-reduction finding. No logical relationship - one is a data pipeline detail, the other a psychological outcome.

claim_a.text: Frontline humanitarian negotiators found process-oriented AI tools that surfaced options and risks more effective than tools providing direct strategy recommendations
claim_a.paper_title: ChatGPT, Don't Tell Me What to Do: Designing AI for Context Analysis in Humanitarian Frontline Negotiations

claim_b.text: Feedback based on zero-shot GPT-4 prompting improved LLM negotiator performance in simulated bargaining but failed to improve human negotiator performance
claim_b.paper_title: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System

label: support
notes: Ma et al. and ACE. Both converge on the insight that what works for AI does not transfer to humans - Ma qualitatively (humans prefer process support), ACE quantitatively (LLM feedback helps LLMs not humans).

claim_a.text: Experienced frontline humanitarian negotiators expressed tolerance for occasional LLM hallucinations, viewing AI errors as manageable with their expertise
claim_a.paper_title: ChatGPT, Don't Tell Me What to Do: Designing AI for Context Analysis in Humanitarian Frontline Negotiations

claim_b.text: Novice negotiators are at greater risk from LLM hallucinations because they lack the domain expertise to identify and counteract AI errors, leading to over-reliance
claim_b.paper_title: ChatGPT, Don't Tell Me What to Do: Designing AI for Context Analysis in Humanitarian Frontline Negotiations

label: nuance
notes: Both from Ma et al. Expertise moderates AI error tolerance. Experts manage errors, novices cannot. Both true simultaneously - not a contradiction.

claim_a.text: Making a high initial offer is positively correlated with negotiation earnings (r=0.47, p<0.001)
claim_a.paper_title: Towards An Autonomous Agent that Provides Automated Feedback on Students' Negotiation Skills

claim_b.text: ACE's annotation scheme coaches users to state their opening price first to anchor the negotiation in a favorable position
claim_b.paper_title: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System

label: support
notes: Johnson et al. empirically validates high initial offers correlate with earnings; ACE builds anchoring into its coaching. Both support the same negotiation principle.

claim_a.text: Trucey's responses were 168% less verbose than ChatGPT's and scored significantly higher on readability (Flesch-Kincaid mean 80.00 vs 68.46)
claim_a.paper_title: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?

claim_b.text: Participants under preparation stress rejected AI's incremental conversational delivery as imposing excessive cognitive load, preferring comprehensive immediately accessible content
claim_b.paper_title: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?

label: nuance
notes: Both from Trucey. Trucey more readable than ChatGPT (quantitative) but users still rejected conversational format (qualitative). Both true, different comparisons.

claim_a.text: The IRP prompting pipeline uses an 8-strategy taxonomy from the Interests-Rights-Power framework to classify and score conflict resolution utterances
claim_a.paper_title: Rehearsal: Simulating Conflict to Teach Conflict Resolution

claim_b.text: Making a high initial offer is positively correlated with negotiation earnings (r=0.47, p<0.001)
claim_b.paper_title: Towards An Autonomous Agent that Provides Automated Feedback on Students' Negotiation Skills

label: unrelated
notes: Rehearsal's IRP taxonomy (a method detail) vs. Johnson's initial-offer correlation (a findings result). Different constructs, no relationship.

claim_a.text: A theory-driven AI coach (Trucey) produced the largest reduction in negotiation-related fear compared to both generic AI and a static handbook (Cohen's d=-0.27 vs. ChatGPT)
claim_a.paper_title: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?

claim_b.text: The static negotiation handbook significantly outperformed both AI conditions on psychological empowerment (PEU), with a medium effect size versus Trucey (d=-0.40, p<0.05)
claim_b.paper_title: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?

label: nuance
notes: Trucey paper. AI wins on fear reduction, handbook wins on empowerment. Different DVs, both true.

claim_a.text: Participants interacting with a theory-driven AI negotiation coach showed significantly higher willingness to initiate negotiations than those using generic AI (beta=12.65, p<0.05) in regression analysis
claim_a.paper_title: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?

claim_b.text: All three coaching interventions produced equivalent improvements in willingness to initiate negotiations, with no significant differences on the Kruskal-Wallis test
claim_b.paper_title: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?

label: nuance
notes: Trucey paper. Adjusted regression vs. unadjusted group comparison. Both findings in the same paper - different analytical methods yield different answers without contradicting.

claim_a.text: Simulated conflict practice with Rehearsal reduced participants' conflict self-efficacy after training, as they gained a realistic understanding of how difficult conflicts are
claim_a.paper_title: Rehearsal: Simulating Conflict to Teach Conflict Resolution

claim_b.text: A theory-driven AI coach achieved the largest reduction in negotiation-related fear, suggesting AI coaching reduces anxiety and psychological barriers
claim_b.paper_title: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?

label: contradiction
notes: Rehearsal vs. Trucey. Rehearsal: self-efficacy decreased after AI practice. Trucey: fear decreased after AI coaching. Opposite directional conclusions on psychological effect of AI-assisted practice.

claim_a.text: Theory-grounded LLM prompting (IRP) significantly outperforms standard instruction-following LLMs for generating realistic conflict simulations
claim_a.paper_title: Rehearsal: Simulating Conflict to Teach Conflict Resolution

claim_b.text: A theory-driven AI coach built on Brett and Thompson's negotiation framework produced significantly better fear reduction than a generic ChatGPT condition using the same underlying model
claim_b.paper_title: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?

label: support
notes: Rehearsal and Trucey. Both show theory grounding produces better outcomes than generic LLM use. Different mechanisms, same conclusion.

claim_a.text: ACE achieved high error detection accuracy (above 0.90 for all categories) but lower F1 scores for qualitative categories like strategic closing (F1=0.54)
claim_a.paper_title: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System

claim_b.text: Automatic metrics derived from negotiation dialogue annotations significantly correlated with negotiation outcomes including initial claim (r=0.47) and agreement time (r=0.59)
claim_b.paper_title: Towards An Autonomous Agent that Provides Automated Feedback on Students' Negotiation Skills

label: support
notes: ACE and Johnson et al. Both validate automated negotiation assessment against ground truth using different approaches. Both support feasibility.

claim_a.text: The Trucey experiment recruited 267 participants through Prolific and randomly assigned them to three conditions in a 2:1:1 ratio
claim_a.paper_title: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?

claim_b.text: Standard instruction-following LLMs are too agreeable to serve as effective conflict practice partners without theory grounding
claim_b.paper_title: Rehearsal: Simulating Conflict to Teach Conflict Resolution

label: unrelated
notes: Trucey recruitment method vs. Rehearsal LLM-agreeableness finding. No logical relationship.

claim_a.text: People do not learn negotiation tactics simply from the experience of bargaining; they require structured feedback and instruction to improve
claim_a.paper_title: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System

claim_b.text: Even without feedback, autonomous agent systems improve negotiation ability, and people are more comfortable learning from automated agents than from other people
claim_b.paper_title: Towards An Autonomous Agent that Provides Automated Feedback on Students' Negotiation Skills

label: nuance
notes: ACE: experience alone insufficient without feedback. Johnson: practice with agents helps even without feedback. Differ in degree not direction - some improvement is not the same as sufficiency.

claim_a.text: Current AI tools in negotiation primarily focus on outcomes and automation, neglecting the process-oriented support that frontline negotiators need
claim_a.paper_title: ChatGPT, Don't Tell Me What to Do: Designing AI for Context Analysis in Humanitarian Frontline Negotiations

claim_b.text: The annotation scheme and feedback method built into ACE is based on American-style negotiation conventions and may not transfer to other cultural contexts
claim_b.paper_title: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System

label: support
notes: Ma et al. and ACE. Both acknowledge current AI negotiation tools are context-narrow, from different angles. Same gap.

claim_a.text: ACE used GPT-4 for its negotiation chat agent and GPT-4o for feedback generation, chosen for speed after expert evaluation
claim_a.paper_title: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System

claim_b.text: Participants who trained with Rehearsal improved their conflict resolution performance in a real unaided conflict after training
claim_b.paper_title: Rehearsal: Simulating Conflict to Teach Conflict Resolution

label: unrelated
notes: ACE model selection (method) vs. Rehearsal transfer finding. No logical relationship.

claim_a.text: Interactive AI coaching is more effective than static resources, as ACE's interactive feedback significantly improved outcomes over non-interactive baselines
claim_a.paper_title: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System

claim_b.text: Participants preferred the static handbook over interactive AI, and it outperformed both AI conditions on usability and psychological empowerment
claim_b.paper_title: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?

label: contradiction
notes: ACE vs. Trucey. ACE: interactivity drives improvement. Trucey: static beats interactive. Opposite conclusions on the value of interactivity in negotiation tools.

claim_a.text: Simulated practice with Rehearsal significantly improved cooperative strategy use compared to a lecture-only control group
claim_a.paper_title: Rehearsal: Simulating Conflict to Teach Conflict Resolution

claim_b.text: Passively reading or watching conflict resolution training material builds skills orthogonal to those built through active simulated practice
claim_b.paper_title: Rehearsal: Simulating Conflict to Teach Conflict Resolution

label: nuance
notes: Both from Rehearsal. Practice beats lecture on application skills, but lecture builds different (orthogonal) skills. Both true - not a ranking contradiction.

claim_a.text: The static handbook outperformed both AI conditions on usability (UMUX) and psychological empowerment in the Trucey study
claim_a.paper_title: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?

claim_b.text: ACE's interactive LLM coaching significantly outperformed both no-feedback and alternative-feedback conditions on objective negotiation outcomes
claim_b.paper_title: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System

label: contradiction
notes: Trucey vs. ACE. Trucey: static non-interactive resource beats interactive AI. ACE: interactive AI beats static/no-feedback. Opposite conclusions on whether interactivity helps.

claim_a.text: Hallucinations in Rehearsal's conflict simulations were mitigated but not eliminated by IRP theory grounding, and simulations occasionally felt overly scripted
claim_a.paper_title: Rehearsal: Simulating Conflict to Teach Conflict Resolution

claim_b.text: Frontline negotiators tolerated occasional LLM hallucinations under time pressure, using expertise to triangulate and verify AI output
claim_b.paper_title: ChatGPT, Don't Tell Me What to Do: Designing AI for Context Analysis in Humanitarian Frontline Negotiations

label: nuance
notes: Rehearsal and Ma et al. Hallucinations exist as a technical limitation AND expert users manage them. Both true, different angles.

claim_a.text: The Rehearsal user study evaluated 40 participants in a between-subjects design where they engaged in an actual conflict with a confederate after training
claim_a.paper_title: Rehearsal: Simulating Conflict to Teach Conflict Resolution

claim_b.text: ACE coaching produced a significant performance improvement with a medium effect size (d=0.38) on objective deal prices
claim_b.paper_title: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System

label: unrelated
notes: Rehearsal study design (method) vs. ACE effect size (finding). No logical relationship.

claim_a.text: Simulated AI practice transfers to real behavior, as Rehearsal participants improved conflict performance in an actual unaided conflict after training
claim_a.paper_title: Rehearsal: Simulating Conflict to Teach Conflict Resolution

claim_b.text: Many frontline negotiators fundamentally rejected AI-mediated rehearsal as inadequate, arguing simulation cannot reproduce the unpredictability and embodied pressure of real negotiations
claim_b.paper_title: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?

label: contradiction
notes: Rehearsal vs. Trucey interviews. Rehearsal: simulated practice transfers to real conflict. Trucey participants: simulation cannot capture real negotiation, rehearsal is inadequate. Opposite conclusions on whether AI simulation transfers.

claim_a.text: Cultural background significantly moderated AI coaching effectiveness in negotiation, with outcomes varying across participant backgrounds
claim_a.paper_title: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?

claim_b.text: ACE's coaching scheme is based on American-style negotiation conventions and may not be as useful in other cultural contexts
claim_b.paper_title: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System

label: support
notes: Trucey and ACE. Both point to culture as a constraint on AI negotiation tool effectiveness. Same conclusion from different angles.

claim_a.text: Post-negotiation feedback is one of the most crucial parts of the learning experience, relating performance to fundamental negotiation principles
claim_a.paper_title: Towards An Autonomous Agent that Provides Automated Feedback on Students' Negotiation Skills

claim_b.text: Simulated practice builds application skills, while passive review of the same material builds orthogonal recognition and recall skills
claim_b.paper_title: Rehearsal: Simulating Conflict to Teach Conflict Resolution

label: nuance
notes: Johnson et al. and Rehearsal. Feedback being crucial (Johnson) and passive review building different skills than practice (Rehearsal) can both be true. Different claims about different learning modes.

claim_a.text: The ACE negotiation agent uses dynamic prompting with an adjustable subjective limit to avoid being a pushover and to give robust counteroffers
claim_a.paper_title: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System

claim_b.text: Cultural background significantly moderated the effectiveness of AI negotiation coaching across participant groups
claim_b.paper_title: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?

label: unrelated
notes: ACE prompting mechanism (method) vs. Trucey cultural moderation (finding). No logical relationship.

claim_a.text: Structured feedback is necessary for learning negotiation, as people do not improve from bargaining experience alone without instruction
claim_a.paper_title: ACE: A LLM-based Negotiation Coaching System

claim_b.text: Autonomous agent systems improve negotiation ability even without any feedback provided to the learner
claim_b.paper_title: Towards An Autonomous Agent that Provides Automated Feedback on Students' Negotiation Skills

label: contradiction
notes: ACE vs. Johnson et al. (citing Lin). ACE: experience alone insufficient, feedback necessary. Johnson: practice with agents improves ability without feedback. Direct conflict on whether feedback is necessary for improvement. (Note: borderline with eval_017's nuance framing - this version states the claims more strongly to be a genuine contradiction.)

claim_a.text: Trucey reduced negotiation fear most among all conditions, but produced only negligible improvement in organizational self-efficacy (delta=+0.05)
claim_a.paper_title: Does AI Coaching Prepare us for Workplace Negotiations?

claim_b.text: Simulated conflict practice with Rehearsal reduced participants' self-efficacy after training as they confronted the real difficulty of conflict
claim_b.paper_title: Rehearsal: Simulating Conflict to Teach Conflict Resolution

label: support
notes: Trucey and Rehearsal. Both find AI-assisted practice does not boost self-efficacy - Trucey negligible, Rehearsal a decrease. Same directional conclusion that AI practice does not build confidence.

claim_a.text: Combining BM25 with dense retrieval raised Recall@100 from 0.71 to 0.88 over dense-only retrieval on the benchmark
claim_a.paper_title: Hybrid Sparse-Dense Retrieval for Open-Domain QA

claim_b.text: Adding a lexical BM25 pass to a dense retriever recovered relevant passages that the embedding model ranked far apart, improving recall on out-of-vocabulary queries
claim_b.paper_title: When Dense Retrieval Misses: A Lexical Safety Net

label: support
notes: Two IR papers independently conclude hybrid lexical+dense beats dense-only on recall. Same direction.

claim_a.text: Increasing embedding dimensionality consistently improved retrieval accuracy across all tested corpora, with the 1536-dim model best
claim_a.paper_title: Scaling Embedding Dimensions for Retrieval

claim_b.text: Retrieval accuracy plateaued beyond 768 dimensions and degraded at very high dimensions as added components captured noise rather than signal
claim_b.paper_title: Diminishing Returns in High-Dimensional Embeddings

label: contradiction
notes: Directly opposing conclusions on whether more embedding dimensions keep improving retrieval.

claim_a.text: Cross-encoder reranking of the top-100 candidates substantially improved Precision@10 over the first-stage retriever
claim_a.paper_title: Reranking for Precision in Two-Stage Retrieval

claim_b.text: Cross-encoder reranking added 400-600ms of latency per query, making it impractical for sub-second interactive search at scale without aggressive candidate pruning
claim_b.paper_title: The Latency Cost of Neural Reranking

label: nuance
notes: Reranking helps precision AND hurts latency. Both true on different axes — not a contradiction.

claim_a.text: The corpus was deduplicated with MinHash LSH at a Jaccard threshold of 0.8 before indexing
claim_a.paper_title: Scaling Embedding Dimensions for Retrieval

claim_b.text: Cross-encoder reranking substantially improved Precision@10 over the first-stage retriever
claim_b.paper_title: Reranking for Precision in Two-Stage Retrieval

label: unrelated
notes: A preprocessing detail vs a reranking finding. No logical relationship.

claim_a.text: Query expansion with generated paraphrases improved Recall@20 for short, underspecified queries by 6 points
claim_a.paper_title: Generative Query Expansion for Sparse Queries

claim_b.text: Pseudo-relevance feedback raised recall most on short queries that lacked sufficient terms to match relevant documents directly
claim_b.paper_title: Revisiting Pseudo-Relevance Feedback

label: support
notes: Both find that enriching short/underspecified queries raises recall. Same direction, different mechanism.

claim_a.text: In-domain fine-tuned bi-encoders matched cross-encoder relevance while retrieving an order of magnitude faster
claim_a.paper_title: Closing the Bi-Encoder Gap with In-Domain Training

claim_b.text: Cross-encoders consistently outperformed bi-encoders on relevance regardless of fine-tuning, because joint query-document attention captures interactions a bi-encoder cannot
claim_b.paper_title: Why Cross-Encoders Still Win on Relevance

label: contradiction
notes: Opposite conclusions on whether fine-tuned bi-encoders can match cross-encoder relevance.

claim_a.text: Chain-of-thought prompting improved accuracy on multi-step arithmetic and symbolic reasoning by a large margin
claim_a.paper_title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning

claim_b.text: Chain-of-thought provided negligible benefit on single-step tasks and occasionally hurt accuracy by introducing spurious intermediate steps
claim_b.paper_title: When Chain-of-Thought Does Not Help

label: nuance
notes: CoT helps multi-step but not single-step tasks. Scope difference, both true — not a contradiction.

claim_a.text: The model was instruction-tuned on 1.5M examples drawn from a mixture of 12 task families
claim_a.paper_title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning

claim_b.text: Chain-of-thought prompting improved accuracy on multi-step arithmetic reasoning
claim_b.paper_title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning

label: unrelated
notes: A training-data detail vs a prompting finding. No logical relationship.

claim_a.text: Larger models hallucinated less because better calibration let them abstain when uncertain
claim_a.paper_title: Scale and Calibration in Language Models

claim_b.text: Larger models hallucinated more confidently, producing fluent but false statements that were harder for users to detect
claim_b.paper_title: Confident Hallucination at Scale

label: contradiction
notes: Opposite directional conclusions on how model scale affects hallucination.

claim_a.text: Self-consistency, sampling multiple reasoning chains and taking the majority answer, improved benchmark accuracy over greedy decoding
claim_a.paper_title: Self-Consistency Improves Chain-of-Thought

claim_b.text: Majority voting over independently sampled reasoning paths raised accuracy on arithmetic and commonsense benchmarks
claim_b.paper_title: Sampling-and-Voting for Reasoning

label: support
notes: Both describe the same mechanism — sample multiple chains, vote — and find it raises accuracy. Same conclusion.

claim_a.text: Retrieval-augmented generation reduced factual hallucination on knowledge-intensive queries by grounding answers in retrieved passages
claim_a.paper_title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge Tasks

claim_b.text: Retrieval augmentation introduced new errors when the retrieved context was irrelevant, as the model deferred to misleading passages over its parametric knowledge
claim_b.paper_title: When Retrieval Hurts: Context Distraction in RAG

label: nuance
notes: RAG reduces hallucination with good context but can add errors with bad context. Both true, conditioned on retrieval quality.

claim_a.text: Evaluation used 5-shot prompting at temperature 0 with a fixed prompt template across all tasks
claim_a.paper_title: Self-Consistency Improves Chain-of-Thought

claim_b.text: Self-consistency sampling improved reasoning accuracy over greedy decoding
claim_b.paper_title: Self-Consistency Improves Chain-of-Thought

label: unrelated
notes: An evaluation-setup detail vs the paper's headline finding. No logical relationship.
